title: The Big Rulebooks
subtitle: A field guide to the major regimes

hook.metaphor: A referee doesn't carry one rulebook — they carry a shelf of them, and they know which applies to which moment of the game. Compliance is the same: there isn't 'the regulation', there's a whole library, each volume guarding a different harm. You don't need to memorise them; you need to recognise which book is open on your desk.
hook.scenario: A health-tech lender in Australia handles patients' medical data, takes card payments, and reports to investors. A colleague asks, 'so which regulation do we follow?' Trick question — how many rulebooks are open at once here?

learn: **Privacy & data** is the most universal shelf: Europe's **GDPR**, India's **DPDP Act**, Australia's **Privacy Act** and **Consumer Data Right (CDR)**, New Zealand's **Privacy Act**. Common thread: get consent, use data fairly, protect it, and respect people's rights over it.

**Financial & prudential** rules keep firms safe and honest: **Basel** standards globally, **APRA**'s prudential standards (Australia), **RBNZ** and **RBI** requirements, and **SOX** (US) for the integrity of financial reporting. Common thread: hold enough buffer, report truthfully, don't blow up.

**Sector-specific** books apply when you touch certain things: **HIPAA** for US health data, **PCI-DSS** for anyone handling card payments, AML laws (the Financial Crime track) for money movement. Common thread: special data, special rules.

**Operational resilience** is the newest shelf: the EU's **DORA**, Australia's **APRA CPS 230**, and similar regimes demanding you keep critical services running through disruption. The key skill across all of them isn't recitation — it's translation: turning 'GDPR exists' into '*so we must* get consent, honour deletion requests, and report breaches within 72 hours.' In our scenario, at least three shelves are open at once (privacy, cards/PCI, financial reporting).
bridge: So at work, you can stop asking 'what's *the* regulation?' and start asking 'which rulebooks are *open* for this activity?' Touching personal data opens privacy law; taking cards opens PCI; moving money opens AML; reporting to investors opens financial-reporting rules. Most real products have several open at once — spotting all of them is half the job.

interactions.title: Regime → what it forces
interactions.prompt: Match each rulebook to what it mainly requires.

pairs.right: Consent, fair use, and protection of personal data
Holding enough capital and managing risk soundly
Integrity and accuracy of financial reporting

interactions.title: Which shelf is open?
interactions.prompt: For each activity, which broad rulebook does it primarily trigger?

items.label: Honouring a customer's request to delete their data
Holding a capital buffer against losses

teachBack: Explain it to a friend in one line: “There's no single 'the regulation' — there's a library: privacy, financial, sector, and resilience rulebooks — and the skill is spotting which ones are open for what you're doing.”

checkpoint.q: GDPR, India's DPDP Act, and Australia's Privacy Act all primarily govern…
checkpoint.explain: They're the privacy/data shelf: consent, fair use, protection, and individuals' rights over their data.

checkpoint.q: PCI-DSS applies to any organisation that…
checkpoint.explain: PCI-DSS is the card-data security standard — touch card payments and it's open on your desk.

checkpoint.q: The most useful skill with any regulation is to…
options: Translate it into 'so we must actually do X'
Wait for the regulator to explain it
checkpoint.explain: Turning law into concrete required actions (consent, breach reporting, capital, etc.) is the heart of compliance.
